title: Guardrails for AI
subtitle: Protecting AI applications from prompt injection and data leakage

steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: AI guardrails are the bouncer and the dress code at a nightclub. The bouncer checks IDs on the way in (prompt validation), and the dress code ensures nobody leaves wearing something they shouldn't (output filtering for PII/secrets).

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
Akamai positions AI guardrails at the edge — between the user and the AI model — using App & API Protector and EdgeWorkers/Functions:

- **Inbound**: Detect and block prompt injection, jailbreak attempts, and adversarial inputs before they reach the model
- **Outbound**: Filter model responses for PII leakage, hallucinated URLs, or prohibited content
- **Rate limiting**: Per-user, per-token throttling to prevent abuse
- **API schema enforcement**: Validate that AI API calls conform to expected structure

The edge is the natural enforcement point — bad requests never reach origin or consume GPU cycles.

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: A customer deploys a customer-service chatbot. Without guardrails, users can prompt-inject to extract training data or system prompts. With Akamai's edge guardrails: AAP catches injection patterns, an EdgeWorker strips PII from responses, and rate limiting prevents one user from monopolising GPU capacity.
"AI guardrails at the edge: block bad prompts inbound, filter sensitive data outbound, rate-limit abuse — all before hitting your model."

quiz.question: Why is the edge the right place for AI guardrails?

options.explain: Cost isn't the primary reason — security and efficiency are.

options.text: Bad requests are blocked before consuming GPU cycles or reaching the model
options.explain: Blocking at the edge saves expensive GPU compute and prevents the model from ever seeing adversarial inputs.
Models can have internal safety, but edge enforcement is the first line of defense.
They complement model-level safety, not replace it.

quiz.question: What are the two directions of AI guardrail enforcement?

options.explain: That's network traffic terminology, not guardrail directions.

options.text: Inbound (prompt validation, injection detection) and outbound (PII filtering, content safety)
options.explain: Guard what goes into the model AND what comes out of it.
Guardrails operate at inference time, not training time.
Guardrails apply regardless of the interface.
